notes.anchor: the day before the Kalends of October
notes.note: Counted inclusively in the Roman manner, this is 30 September 50 BC; Atticus's letter and Batonius's alarming news reached Cicero at Ephesus the day before he embarked for Greece.

notes.anchor: the praetors-designate, the tribune of the plebs Cassius, and the consul Lentulus
notes.note: The men elected for 49 BC: Q. Cassius Longinus (tribune) and L. Cornelius Lentulus Crus (consul-designate, here loosely called 'consul'). Batonius's report that Caesar had them in his pay is an early sign of the alignments that would harden into civil war within months.

notes.anchor: Caesar will on no account dismiss his army
notes.note: The central crisis of 50-49 BC: the Senate demanded Caesar lay down his Gallic command before standing for the consulship, while he refused to disband his legions; his keeping the army was the act that precipitated the crossing of the Rubicon.
The etesiae were the steady north-northwest summer winds of the Aegean; blowing against a westbound voyage, they cost Cicero a full twenty days on the homeward run from Ephesus.

An aphractus was a light, undecked galley (Greek aphraktos, 'unfenced'); the Rhodians were famous shipbuilders, but their open craft were poorly suited to the heavy seas Cicero faced and slowed his crossing.

Cicero's friends were pressing him to seek a triumph for his Cilician campaign; he frets that his predecessor in Syria, Bibulus, who never left camp, was angling for one too, which would make Cicero's own claim look grasping.
